planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; plants establish readily along streambanks, woodland floors, and shaded rain garden margins where moisture is consistent.

directions: Set divisions into consistently moist, organically rich soil in partial to full shade in spring, pressing crowns to soil level and watering well to eliminate air pockets around the roots. Reflexed sedge is a delicate, fine-textured native of southeastern US woodlands that forms tidy, low clumps ideal for filling shaded understory areas, moist woodland edges, and stream banks where taller plants struggle. The reflexed seed spikes that give this sedge its name arch gracefully outward at maturity, adding subtle texture to the woodland floor from late spring through summer. Divide established clumps every three to four years in early spring to maintain vigorous growth and expand the planting.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into moist to moderately dry soil in partial to full shade; space plugs 9–15 inches apart to allow the mat-forming habit to knit together as a low-maintenance groundcover.

directions: Plant divisions in spring into well-drained to moderately moist soil beneath oaks, pines, or other high-canopy trees, spacing plugs 9–15 inches apart and watering in thoroughly to settle the roots. Florida sedge is an evergreen, fine-textured native of the southeastern coastal plain that spreads gradually by short rhizomes to form a dense, low-maintenance mat just four to ten inches tall, making it one of the best native alternatives to turfgrass in shaded Florida and Gulf Coast landscapes. Once established, it requires little supplemental irrigation and no fertilizer, thriving in the naturally lean, acidic soils found under longleaf pine and live oak canopies. Mow or shear lightly once a year in late winter if a tidier appearance is desired, then allow fresh foliage to flush before the growing season begins.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring after the last frost into boggy soil or directly into shallow water up to six inches deep; plants establish quickly in warm temperatures and benefit from full sun to light shade.

directions: Set divisions into boggy margins, rain garden bottoms, or pond shelves in spring once nighttime temperatures remain above 50°F, placing crowns at water level or submerging in up to six inches of standing water. Haspan flatsedge produces slender, upright stems topped with delicate, star-burst umbels that resemble miniature papyrus, providing fine-textured vertical interest in water gardens and naturalistic wet-meadow plantings across the Southeast and tropics. In zones 8 and above, roots are typically cold-hardy even when frost kills the foliage; mulch the crown heavily in fall at the cooler edge of its range to improve winter survival. Divide congested clumps every two to three years in spring to maintain the open, airy habit that makes this sedge most ornamentally effective.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring onto a lightly raked, open seedbed in partial shade; press seed into the soil surface without deep covering and keep the area evenly moist until germination occurs in two to three weeks.

directions: Scatter seed in early spring onto a lightly disturbed, open soil surface in partial shade, pressing seeds into contact with the soil and watering gently to maintain moisture through germination. Tapered rosette panicgrass is a native of eastern North America's woodland edges and sandy clearings that forms a distinctive flat basal rosette of soft, hairy leaves in fall and winter, then sends up flowering culms 12–24 inches tall in late spring. It thrives in the dappled light of open woodlands and forest margins where few other grasses establish, making it a valuable groundcover and wildlife forage grass in naturalistic plantings. Allow the plant to self-sow freely, as seedlings are easy to identify by their characteristic winter rosette and help naturalize shaded areas over time.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring onto bare, sandy, or gravelly soil in full sun; press seed lightly into the surface and keep the site moist until seedlings emerge, then reduce watering as plants develop their deep root system.

directions: Broadcast seed onto a loose, infertile sandy or gravelly seedbed in full sun in early spring, pressing it lightly into the soil surface and watering until seedlings establish over the first few weeks. Sand reed grass is a deep-rooted native of the central Great Plains and interior dune systems, where its extensive rhizome network anchors shifting sand and withstands the full force of plains wind and drought with ease. Plants produce graceful, feathery seed plumes in late summer that persist through winter, providing outstanding visual interest and valuable wildlife cover in prairie restorations, dry meadows, and stabilized dune plantings. Avoid fertilizing or irrigating once established, as lean, dry conditions produce the strongest roots and the most attractive, wind-resilient growth.
